text: What is the basic unit of CPU utilization that forms the foundation of multithreaded computer systems?
info: A thread is the basic unit of CPU utilization that enables multiple parts of a process to run concurrently within the same process.

answers.text: Process, which is the primary unit of resource allocation
Thread, which is a sub-unit within a process that uses the CPU
Interrupt, which is used to preempt CPU execution
Kernel, which is the core of the operating system

text: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of multithreaded programming?
info: The main benefits of multithreading are: Responsiveness, Resource Sharing, Economy (threads are cheaper to create than processes), and Scalability on multicore systems.

answers.text: Responsiveness, especially for user interfaces
Resource Sharing, which is simpler than IPC
Improved Security from thread isolation
Economy, because threads are faster to create and switch than processes

text: What is the key difference between concurrency and parallelism?
info: Parallelism means multiple tasks are executed simultaneously on multiple processing units. Concurrency means multiple tasks make progress, possibly by interleaving on a single processing unit.

answers.text: Concurrency is only for single-core systems, while Parallelism is only for multi-core systems
Parallelism executes multiple tasks truly simultaneously, while Concurrency makes tasks progress without necessarily being simultaneous
Concurrency refers to data partitioning, while Parallelism refers to task partitioning
Concurrency always requires synchronization, while Parallelism does not

text: In the context of multicore programming, what does data parallelism mean?
info: Data parallelism involves distributing subsets of the same data across multiple cores, where each core performs the same operation on its portion of the data.

answers.text: Distributing different threads to perform different tasks on different cores
Distributing subsets of the same data across multiple cores, performing the same operation on each subset
Running multiple processes in parallel using the same dataset
Dividing code into sections and having each core run a different section

text: Which multithreading model maps multiple user-level threads to a single kernel thread, causing all threads to block if one blocks?
info: The Many-to-One model maps multiple user threads to a single kernel thread. Its drawback is that if any thread blocks, all threads block, and it cannot exploit parallelism on multicore systems.

answers.text: One-to-One, which provides maximum parallelism
Many-to-Many, which uses many user and kernel threads
Many-to-One, which was used in Solaris Green Threads
Two-level model, which includes special thread binding

text: Which of the following operating systems uses the One-to-One multithreading model?
info: The One-to-One model is used by Windows, Linux, and Solaris 9 and above, where each user-level thread is mapped directly to one kernel thread.

answers.text: Solaris Green Threads and GNU Portable Threads
Windows, Linux, and Solaris 9 and above
IRIX, HP-UX, and older FreeBSD versions
macOS and iOS before version 10.6

text: What is a key characteristic of user-level threads compared to kernel-level threads?
info: User-level threads are managed by a user-level thread library without kernel involvement, making thread switching very fast. However, if a thread blocks, the entire process may block.

answers.text: User-level threads are managed by the kernel and can fully exploit parallelism
User-level threads are managed by a user-level library without going through the kernel
User-level threads have higher overhead than kernel threads because they require syscalls
User-level threads cannot work with kernel threads

text: What is the name of the POSIX standard API for creating and managing threads commonly used in UNIX systems?
info: Pthreads (POSIX threads) is the IEEE 1003.1c standard thread API commonly found in UNIX systems such as Solaris, Linux, and Mac OS X.

answers.text: Win32 Thread API
Java Thread API (java.lang.Thread)
Pthreads (POSIX threads)
OpenMP Runtime Library

text: By which component are Java threads managed, and which threading model is generally used?
info: Java threads are managed by the JVM (Java Virtual Machine) and generally use the threading model supported by the underlying OS (typically one-to-one).

answers.text: JVM uses one-to-one model on most operating systems
Java Compiler manages threads using always a many-to-one model
Java Class Loader uses a many-to-many model for better performance
Java Runtime Environment (JRE) uses only user-level threads

text: What are the ways to create a thread in Java?
info: In Java, threads can be created by extending the Thread class or by implementing the Runnable interface and passing it to the Thread constructor.

answers.text: Extend Thread class or Implement Runnable interface
Use fork() in java.lang.Process
Use clone() in java.lang.Object
Use JNI to call pthread_create() only

text: What does Implicit threading mean?
info: Implicit threading is the creation and management of threads by the compiler or run-time libraries instead of the programmer, reducing complexity.

answers.text: The programmer must explicitly write code to create and manage threads every time
The compiler and run-time libraries automatically create and manage threads
The operating system hides threads from the user
Programs cannot create threads and must rely on hardware only

text: Which of the following is an Implicit threading technique?
info: Implicit threading techniques discussed in the content include Thread Pools, OpenMP, Grand Central Dispatch (GCD), and others such as Intel TBB and java.util.concurrent.

answers.text: Pthreads, which requires manually calling pthread_create
Windows API CreateThread
Thread Pools that have threads ready and waiting to accept jobs from a queue
fork() and exec() for creating processes

text: What are the advantages of a Thread pool?
info: Thread pools reduce the overhead of creating new threads each time, limit the number of threads in the system, and separate the task from the thread creation mechanism for flexible scheduling.

answers.text: Reduce thread creation overhead, limit the number of threads, and separate tasks from the thread creation mechanism
Allow unlimited thread creation for faster responses
Guarantee that threads always run in real-time
Eliminate the need for synchronization between threads

text: What problem arises when calling fork() in a multithreaded program?
info: In some UNIX variants there are two versions of fork(): one that duplicates only the calling thread, and another that duplicates all threads, depending on the implementation.

answers.text: fork() will copy only the calling thread, causing other threads to disappear
fork() may copy only the calling thread or all threads, depending on the implementation
fork() cannot be used if there is more than one thread
fork() always automatically copies all threads

text: How does deferred cancellation of a thread work?
info: Deferred cancellation allows the target thread to periodically check whether it should be cancelled (e.g., at cancellation points), enabling the thread to perform cleanup before terminating.

answers.text: Cancel the thread immediately without waiting (asynchronous)
Allow the thread to check periodically and cancel when it reaches a cancellation point
Must call pthread_join() before cancellation is possible
The thread cannot be cancelled in this mode

text: In Pthreads, what happens when a thread's cancellation type is set to asynchronous?
info: Asynchronous cancellation terminates the target thread immediately without waiting for a cancellation point, which may cause resources to not be released properly.

answers.text: The thread is cancelled immediately without waiting for a cancellation point
The thread is cancelled only when it calls pthread_testcancel()
The thread is suspended until pthread_join() is called
Setting asynchronous has no effect

text: What is the benefit of Thread-local storage (TLS)?
info: TLS allows each thread to have its own private storage area that is not shared between threads, reducing race condition problems.

answers.text: Used for safely sharing data between threads
Used for storing each thread's own private data, separate from others
Used for storing process-wide data so all threads can access it
Used as a buffer for faster I/O

text: In Windows systems, what components does each thread consist of?
info: A Windows thread consists of a thread ID, register set, user stack, kernel stack, and a private storage area for run-time libraries and DLLs.

answers.text: Thread ID, register set, user stack, kernel stack, private storage
Only the program counter and stack pointer
Only the PCB (Process Control Block) and TCB (Thread Control Block)
Only the user stack and kernel stack with no thread ID

text: What are the main data structures for threads in Windows?
info: Windows has ETHREAD (executive thread block), KTHREAD (kernel thread block), and TEB (thread environment block) for managing thread data.

answers.text: ETHREAD, KTHREAD, TEB
PCB, TCB, UCB (User Control Block)
TSS (Task State Segment), GDT, LDT
Only Thread Control Block (TCB)

text: In Linux, which system call is used to create a thread and how does it work?
info: Linux uses the clone() system call, which allows the child task to share the parent's address space via flags such as CLONE_VM, CLONE_FILES, etc.

answers.text: fork(), which always creates a new process and cannot share address space
clone(), which can be configured to share the parent's address space with the child
pthread_create(), which is a direct system call in Linux
exec(), which is used to load a new program

text: In Linux, what does the CLONE_VM flag used with clone() mean?
info: CLONE_VM causes the child task to share the same memory space as the parent, which is a key characteristic of threads.

answers.text: Share file-system information (e.g., root, cwd)
Share the same address space (memory space)
Share signal handlers
Share the open file descriptor table

text: What are the advantages of using the many-to-many multithreading model?
info: The Many-to-Many model allows the system to create many user threads without being tied to many kernel threads, and can still exploit parallelism on multicore systems.

answers.text: Reduces kernel thread creation overhead and increases concurrency
Guarantees that thread blocking will not affect other threads
Forces each user thread to remain bound to a kernel thread at all times
Requires no scheduler at all

text: What is the special feature of the Two-level model (similar to M:M)?
info: The Two-level model is similar to many-to-many but adds the ability to bind a user thread directly to a specific kernel thread (bound thread) for performance.

answers.text: Allows some user threads to be bound to a specific kernel thread
Runs all user threads on a single kernel thread
Does not support parallelism on multicore systems
Requires special hardware support only

text: What do Scheduler Activations relate to?
info: Scheduler activations are a mechanism for communication between the user-level thread library and the kernel to manage scheduling efficiently, reducing problems when a thread blocks.

answers.text: Managing scheduling between user-level threads and kernel threads
Automatically activating the scheduler in the kernel
Disabling preemptive scheduling for stability
Statically ordering threads by priority

text: Which statement about OpenMP is correct?
info: OpenMP is a set of compiler directives and libraries for parallel programming, especially on shared memory systems, and is one of the implicit threading techniques.

answers.text: It is an API for creating threads specifically in Java
It is a set of compiler directives and libraries for parallel programming
It is a threading model used exclusively in Windows
It is a system call for creating lightweight processes

text: Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) was developed for which operating system?
info: GCD was developed by Apple for macOS and iOS. It is an implicit threading technique that uses blocks and dispatch queues.

answers.text: Windows 10 and above
Linux (GNU/Linux)
macOS and iOS
Solaris 11

text: Why is creating a thread more economical than creating a process?
info: Threads use fewer resources because they share the memory space and other resources of the same process, without needing full copy-on-write.

answers.text: Because threads do not need a separate stack
Because threads share the address space and resources of the process
Because threads do not require interrupt handling
Because threads do not use memory

text: What are the challenges of multicore programming as described in the content?
info: The challenges include: dividing activities, balancing, data splitting, managing data dependency, testing, and debugging.

answers.text: Dividing activities, Balance, Data splitting, Data dependency, Testing and debugging
Only thread creation and locking
Only memory management and cache coherency
No challenges at all, because the compiler handles everything

text: In Windows systems, where is the TEB (Thread Environment Block) stored?
info: The TEB (Thread Environment Block) stores the thread ID, user-mode stack, and thread-local storage, and resides in user space.

answers.text: Kernel space
User space
Registry
ROM (read-only memory)

text: What is the primary role of KTHREAD (Kernel Thread Block) in Windows?
info: KTHREAD stores scheduling and synchronization data, the kernel-mode stack, and a pointer to the TEB, residing in kernel space.

answers.text: Stores scheduling and synchronization information including the kernel stack
Stores all data about the process to which this thread belongs
Stores user-mode stack and thread-local storage
Stores information about dynamic link libraries (DLLs)

text: What does Linux call threads?
info: Linux refers to threads as 'tasks' and uses the struct task_struct to manage them.

answers.text: Processes
Tasks
Threads
Kernel threads

text: Which operating system used the many-to-many model in versions prior to 9?
info: Solaris before version 9 used the many-to-many model (also called the MxN model).

answers.text: Windows XP
Linux kernel 2.4
Solaris 8 and earlier
macOS 10.5 (Leopard)

text: How does signal handling in multithreaded programs become more complex?
info: Signals can be synchronous (generated by the thread itself) or asynchronous (sent from another thread or process), requiring decisions about which thread should receive the signal.

answers.text: Must determine which thread receives the signal and handle both sync/async signals
Signals cannot be used in multithreaded programs
All signals are delivered only to the main thread
No added complexity since it is handled the same as in single-threaded programs

text: Which of the following is an example of implicit threading used in Java?
info: The java.util.concurrent package is an example of implicit threading in Java, providing thread pools, executors, and utilities.

answers.text: java.util.concurrent (Executors, ThreadPoolExecutor)
javax.swing.SwingUtilities.invokeLater()
java.lang.Thread directly
java.io.InputStreamReader

text: What are the characteristics of threads in the Windows API compared to threading models?
info: Windows uses one-to-one mapping, where each thread has a context consisting of a register set, stacks, and private storage.

answers.text: Uses one-to-one mapping with register set, stacks, and private storage
Uses many-to-one mapping to reduce overhead
Uses many-to-many mapping through ThreadFiber
Has no private storage and must use global variables instead

text: In Linux, which flag used with clone() is for sharing signal handlers?
info: CLONE_SIGHAND is the flag that causes the child task to share signal handlers with the parent.

answers.text: CLONE_FS
CLONE_VM
CLONE_SIGHAND
CLONE_FILES

text: What is the benefit of implicit threading in terms of program correctness?
info: As the number of threads increases, managing threads explicitly makes it harder to maintain correctness. Implicit threading reduces complexity and the chance of errors.

answers.text: Reduces complexity and lowers the chance of errors from manually managing threads
Always makes the program run faster
Eliminates the need for any synchronization
Guarantees that no deadlock will occur

text: In Windows, what threading model does the ThreadFiber package use?
info: ThreadFiber in Windows is an implementation of the many-to-many model, allowing user threads to manage fibers, which are lightweight units.

answers.text: One-to-One (like regular threads)
Many-to-One (multiple fibers on a single thread only)
Many-to-Many (multiple fibers on multiple threads)
Two-level model that binds a fiber to a specific thread

text: When using Pthreads, what is the effect of setting the cancellation state to PTHREAD_CANCEL_DISABLE?
info: If cancellation state is disabled, the thread will not be cancelled until it is enabled again. The cancellation request will remain pending.

answers.text: The thread is cancelled immediately (asynchronous cancellation)
The thread will not be cancelled until cancellation is enabled and a cancellation point is reached
The thread will be suspended until pthread_join() is called
The program will immediately cause a segmentation fault

text: What are the advantages of using thread pools?
info: Thread pools reduce thread creation overhead, limit the number of threads, and separate the task from the thread creation mechanism.

answers.text: Reduce overhead, limit thread count, and separate tasks from thread creation mechanism
Allow unlimited thread creation for faster responses
Guarantee that threads always run in real-time
Eliminate the need for synchronization between threads

text: In Linux, what is the role of struct task_struct?
info: struct task_struct is the main data structure that holds information about a task (process/thread), including both shared and unique data for each task.

answers.text: Stores process/thread data and points to other data structures
Stores only the PID (process identifier)
Stores only memory maps and page tables
Stores only signal handlers and pending signals

text: What does Scalability mean in the context of multithreading?
info: Scalability refers to the ability of a process to efficiently leverage multiprocessor architecture as the number of cores increases.

answers.text: The ability to take advantage of multiple processors
The ability to expand the number of threads without limit
The ability to expand the amount of memory used
The ability to support multiple users simultaneously

text: What does task parallelism programming look like?
info: Task parallelism involves distributing threads across cores where each thread performs a unique, different operation.

answers.text: Distribute the same dataset to multiple cores and perform the same operation
Distribute threads where each thread performs a different operation
Run multiple programs simultaneously on the same core
Use a single thread to perform multiple tasks by interleaving

text: In Windows, what information does ETHREAD (Executive Thread Block) store?
info: ETHREAD stores a pointer to the process the thread belongs to, a pointer to the KTHREAD, and other data used by the executive.

answers.text: Pointer to the process and to KTHREAD
Only thread ID and priority
User-mode stack and thread-local storage
Kernel-mode stack and trap frame

text: What is the impact of using asynchronous cancellation without cleanup preparation?
info: Asynchronous cancellation may cause resources (e.g., memory, file handles) not to be released, leading to memory leaks or corrupted state.

answers.text: May cause resource leaks and inconsistent state
No impact because the kernel handles cleanup automatically
The program will run faster
The thread will be suspended instead of cancelled

text: In Pthreads, what does the function pthread_testcancel() do?
info: pthread_testcancel() creates a cancellation point so that the thread can check if there is a pending cancellation request. If there is, the thread will be cancelled at that point.

answers.text: Tests whether the thread has already been cancelled, without actually cancelling it
Creates a cancellation point for the thread to check and cancel if a request is pending
Cancels the thread immediately without waiting
Disables cancellation for this thread

text: Why do most modern operating systems choose to use the one-to-one model?
info: The one-to-one model provides full parallelism on multicore systems and makes thread management simpler, despite slightly more overhead than many-to-one.

answers.text: To achieve true parallelism on multicore systems and reduce management complexity
To minimize thread creation overhead
To be compatible with the Java Virtual Machine only
Because the many-to-one model cannot run on multicore systems

text: In Linux, when a child is created with clone() using CLONE_FILES, how are file descriptors shared between parent and child?
info: CLONE_FILES causes the child and parent to share the same set of open files.

answers.text: They share the same set of open files
They have completely separate open files
The child cannot open any files
All open files are closed after clone

text: What is the advantage of the many-to-many model over one-to-one?
info: The many-to-many model allows many user threads without requiring an equal number of kernel threads, reducing overhead and improving scalability.

answers.text: Reduces overhead from creating many kernel threads
Provides higher parallelism than one-to-one
Is easier to implement in the kernel
Guarantees that thread blocking will have no impact

text: In Windows, what does the Thread Environment Block (TEB) store?
info: TEB stores the thread ID, user-mode stack, thread-local storage, and data used by run-time libraries and DLLs.

answers.text: Thread ID, user-mode stack, thread-local storage
Kernel-mode stack and scheduling parameters
Pointer to KTHREAD and ETHREAD
Only the register set and instruction pointer
